prompt:

subject: reading
prompt:
Which choice best states the main purpose of the text?
The following text is from Anne Spencer's 1922 poem "Translation." We trekked into a far country,
My friend and I.
Our deeper content was never spoken,
But each knew all the other said.
He told me how calm his soul was laid
By the lack of anvil and strife.
"The wooing kestrel," I said, "mutes his mating-note
To please the harmony of this sweet silence."
passage:
The following text is from Anne Spencer's 1922 poem "Translation." We trekked into a far country,
My friend and I.
Our deeper content was never spoken,
But each knew all the other said.
He told me how calm his soul was laid
By the lack of anvil and strife.
"The wooing kestrel," I said, "mutes his mating-note
To please the harmony of this sweet silence."
questionText: Which choice best states the main purpose of the text?

choices.text: It lists the sights that the speaker and her friend encounter while on a long hike.
It conveys the speaker's awareness that she is both attracted to and repelled by nature.
It describes differences between the speaker's translation of a literary work and another translation of the same work.
It depicts a feeling of unspoken connection between the speaker and her friend.

subject: reading
prompt:
Both texts discuss research on which topic?
Text 1
From about 985 CE to the early 1400s, when they mysteriously abandoned the region, Norse settlers occupied part of Greenland. Many researchers cite increasingly lower temperatures to explain why the Norse fled. The researchers reason that the Norse settlers arrived in the region while the climate was warm and were able to remain there until temperatures declined during the Little Ice Age, which occurred between the 14th and 19th centuries.
Text 2
William Daniels, Boyang Zhao, and colleagues analyzed sediment samples from a lake in Greenland close to the Norse settlement. This allowed them to reconstruct the area's climate history. The researchers found no evidence of substantial temperature changes during the settlement period. They argue that instead the region experienced a persistent drought, which played a more important role in forcing the Norse out of the area than did increasingly lower temperatures.
passage:
Text 1
From about 985 CE to the early 1400s, when they mysteriously abandoned the region, Norse settlers occupied part of Greenland. Many researchers cite increasingly lower temperatures to explain why the Norse fled. The researchers reason that the Norse settlers arrived in the region while the climate was warm and were able to remain there until temperatures declined during the Little Ice Age, which occurred between the 14th and 19th centuries.
Text 2
William Daniels, Boyang Zhao, and colleagues analyzed sediment samples from a lake in Greenland close to the Norse settlement. This allowed them to reconstruct the area's climate history. The researchers found no evidence of substantial temperature changes during the settlement period. They argue that instead the region experienced a persistent drought, which played a more important role in forcing the Norse out of the area than did increasingly lower temperatures.
questionText: Both texts discuss research on which topic?

choices.text: The first artifacts found at a Norse settlement
The abandonment of the Norse settlement in Greenland
The history of drought in Greenland
The causes of the Little Ice Age

subject: reading
prompt:
According to the text, how do historians view pachuca style?
During the World War II era, some Mexican American women adopted a striking new look called *pachuca style*. They wore altered men's jackets or zoot suits (wide-legged, long-coated suits) and dramatic makeup, and they combed their hair into high, rounded shapes. Some people criticized *pachuca style*, saying it was dangerous and women should dress traditionally. But historians see things differently. They see *pachuca style* as a form of rebellion against the era's rigid social expectations for women. They say that it showed a desire for self-expression and freedom on the part of women who adopted the style.
passage: During the World War II era, some Mexican American women adopted a striking new look called *pachuca style*. They wore altered men's jackets or zoot suits (wide-legged, long-coated suits) and dramatic makeup, and they combed their hair into high, rounded shapes. Some people criticized *pachuca style*, saying it was dangerous and women should dress traditionally. But historians see things differently. They see *pachuca style* as a form of rebellion against the era's rigid social expectations for women. They say that it showed a desire for self-expression and freedom on the part of women who adopted the style.
questionText: According to the text, how do historians view pachuca style?

choices.text: They think that pachuca style was similar to other fashion trends that different groups of women adopted in the same period.
They think that pachuca style was celebrated because it enabled some Mexican American women to show their support for the United States during World War II.
They think that pachuca style was such a popular trend that it continues to influence fashion in the United States to the present day.
They think that pachuca style was a way for some Mexican American women to express themselves and resist strict social expectations.

subject: reading
prompt:
Which choice best states the main idea of the text?
Community science involves professional scientists collaborating with members of the public to study a topic. This approach to research can promote community engagement by building youth confidence in the sciences. It's also very effective because it greatly increases the amount of data that can be collected, such as when biologist Abbigail Merrill and colleagues studied how butterfly color relates to flower choice and used findings reported by hundreds of students and community members in northwestern Arkansas.
passage: Community science involves professional scientists collaborating with members of the public to study a topic. This approach to research can promote community engagement by building youth confidence in the sciences. It's also very effective because it greatly increases the amount of data that can be collected, such as when biologist Abbigail Merrill and colleagues studied how butterfly color relates to flower choice and used findings reported by hundreds of students and community members in northwestern Arkansas.
questionText: Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.text: A community science approach can increase youth confidence in learning science and generate large amounts of data, benefiting both communities and researchers.
A community science approach can benefit communities by increasing youth confidence in learning science, but it's less likely to help researchers obtain accurate data.
Abbigail Merrill and colleagues used a community science approach in their butterfly research and were surprised at the effect this had on youth confidence in learning science.
A community science approach allows researchers to collect significantly more data even though it's unlikely to have much of an effect on youth confidence in learning science.

subject: reading
prompt:
Which quotation from a historian best supports the student's claim?
A student is writing a research paper on the history of irrigation in the southwestern United States, situating the development of the Bear River Reservoir (a man-made body of water created in Amador County, California, in 1900) in a larger historical context. The student claims that some modern-day irrigation methods used in southwestern states, such as California, Arizona, and New Mexico, have a lineage that long predates the completion of the Bear River Reservoir.
passage: A student is writing a research paper on the history of irrigation in the southwestern United States, situating the development of the Bear River Reservoir (a man-made body of water created in Amador County, California, in 1900) in a larger historical context. The student claims that some modern-day irrigation methods used in southwestern states, such as California, Arizona, and New Mexico, have a lineage that long predates the completion of the Bear River Reservoir.
questionText: Which quotation from a historian best supports the student's claim?

choices.text: "In the decades following the Second World War, irrigation of arid areas for agriculture increased to such a degree that it now accounts for roughly 70% of the world's water usage."
"The importance of the development of irrigation infrastructure in the American Southwest today cannot be overstated, since it is the most common means of conveying water for food production."

"Sprinkler irrigation systems, which were developed in the late 20th century, are a convenient technique of irrigating that makes use of machinery in order to spray water in all directions. Many areas of the Southwestern United States are currently irrigated by this method."

"The irrigation system developed by the Hohokam people in what is now central Arizona in the 7th century CE was simple but made use of hydraulic engineering design principles that are still utilized by today's engineers."

subject: reading
prompt:
Which finding, if true, would most directly support Valerio and colleagues' conclusion?
Eastern Maine's Washington County is among the most rural counties in the United States: the US Census Bureau classified it as 92.4% rural in 2010. Researchers have struggled to recruit residents of counties like Washington for inclusion in studies. Noting this problem, Melissa Valerio and colleagues tested two recruitment techniques in rural counties: purposive sampling, in which researchers contact many potential participants who have the characteristics desired for a given study, and snowball sampling, in which researchers recruit a few people with the desired characteristics and ask them to recruit additional participants. Valerio and colleagues concluded that the latter approach may be superior.
passage: Eastern Maine's Washington County is among the most rural counties in the United States: the US Census Bureau classified it as 92.4% rural in 2010. Researchers have struggled to recruit residents of counties like Washington for inclusion in studies. Noting this problem, Melissa Valerio and colleagues tested two recruitment techniques in rural counties: purposive sampling, in which researchers contact many potential participants who have the characteristics desired for a given study, and snowball sampling, in which researchers recruit a few people with the desired characteristics and ask them to recruit additional participants. Valerio and colleagues concluded that the latter approach may be superior.
questionText: Which finding, if true, would most directly support Valerio and colleagues' conclusion?

choices.text: Snowball sampling yielded significantly more participants with the desired characteristics than purposive sampling did.
Snowball sampling yielded significantly more participants than would be expected if researchers contacted people without regard to those people's characteristics.
Participants recruited through snowball sampling were more likely to have taken part in previous studies than were participants recruited through purposive sampling.
Most participants recruited through snowball sampling had different characteristics than the initial participants who recruited them.

subject: reading
prompt:
Which choice best states the function of the underlined portion in the text as a whole?
Macaque monkeys and falcons see in three dimensions (3D) by combining two images in their brains, one from each eye. This produces a sense of depth, helping the animals judge how close or far away an object is. Researchers have investigated 3D vision in praying mantises as well. In one study, Vivek Nityananda and his team fitted mantises' faces with two different color filters, one covering each eye, <u>much like the filters in 3D glasses once worn at movies</u>. By observing the mantises' reaction to projected images, the team confirmed that mantises do indeed have 3D vision, but it's unlike that of other animals.

subject: reading
prompt:
According to the text, what change coincided with Don Jerónimo becoming a patron of the theater?
The following text is adapted from Armando Palacio Valdés's short story "The Love of Clotilde," originally published in Spanish in 1884.
It was said that in [Don Jerónimo's] youth he once wrote a play which won him nothing but hisses and free entry for life behind the scenes of the theaters. Whether resigned or not to the verdict of the public, he ceased to write plays and assumed instead the nobler rôle of patron to unrecognized authors and artists and to ruined managers.
Any youth from the provinces who arrived in Madrid with a drama in his pocket could take no surer road to seeing it produced than that which led to the home of Don Jerónimo. One and all, he received them with open arms, the good and the bad alike.

Whether resigned or not to the verdict of the public, he ceased to write plays and assumed instead the nobler rôle of patron to unrecognized authors and artists and to ruined managers.
Any youth from the provinces who arrived in Madrid with a drama in his pocket could take no surer road to seeing it produced than that which led to the home of Don Jerónimo. One and all, he received them with open arms, the good and the bad alike.

choices.text: He achieved public recognition for a poem he had written earlier.

subject: reading
prompt:
Based on the text, what can most reasonably be concluded about krill consumption among pygmy right and northern right whales?
Like all species of baleen whales, the pygmy right whale feeds on tiny creatures known as krill by filtering water through bristle-like keratin structures called baleen plates. In this way, baleen whales can eat up to 30 percent of their total mass per day. And while no one would call the pygmy right whale small—it can have a mass as high as 3,500 kg—it is one of the smaller baleen whales and is much smaller than the northern right whale, which can weigh a whopping 80,000 kg and consume as much as 24,000 kg of krill per day.
passage: Like all species of baleen whales, the pygmy right whale feeds on tiny creatures known as krill by filtering water through bristle-like keratin structures called baleen plates. In this way, baleen whales can eat up to 30 percent of their total mass per day. And while no one would call the pygmy right whale small—it can have a mass as high as 3,500 kg—it is one of the smaller baleen whales and is much smaller than the northern right whale, which can weigh a whopping 80,000 kg and consume as much as 24,000 kg of krill per day.
questionText: Based on the text, what can most reasonably be concluded about krill consumption among pygmy right and northern right whales?

choices.text: The quantities of krill consumed by northern right whales has made it difficult for pygmy right whales to find sufficient food.
Both the pygmy right whale and the northern right whale can eat as much as 24,000 kg of krill per day.
The northern right whale is able to eat more krill per day than the pygmy right whale is.
Most baleen whales include krill in their diets, but the pygmy right whale is less likely than the northern right whale to do so.

subject: reading
prompt:
Based on the texts, how would the author of Text 1 most likely respond to the claim about the potential benefits of Costa and colleagues' research in Text 2?
Text 1
Attempts to automate classification of music into genres have not been very successful. It is also unclear whether categorizing music by genre is useful, since genre categories are ambiguous, subjective, and simplistic. As Jin Ha Lee and Anh Thu Nguyen argue in their study of the South Korean band BTS, relationships between pieces of music may be best understood with concepts other than genre.
Text 2
Gaúcha is a genre of music originally from Brazil that shares some harmonic and rhythmic similarities with the bachata genre. Automated genre classification systems typically struggle to draw distinctions in situations like this, but Yandre Costa and colleagues solved that problem by converting sound to images and having computers compare features of those images. Their approach could improve genre classification, which could have many benefits for users.

Text 1
Attempts to automate classification of music into genres have not been very successful. Text 2
Gaúcha is a genre of music originally from Brazil that shares some harmonic and rhythmic similarities with the bachata genre. Automated genre classification systems typically struggle to draw distinctions in situations like this, but Yandre Costa and colleagues solved that problem by converting sound to images and having computers compare features of those images. Their approach could improve genre classification, which could have many benefits for users.

questionText: Which quotation from *Life Among the Paiutes* most effectively illustrates the claim?

choices.text: "That same fall, after my grandfather came home, he told my father to take charge of his people and hold the tribe, as he was going back to California with as many of his people as he could get to go with him."

"Oh, how my heart jumped when I heard a noise close by. It was a horse running towards us. We had to lie down close to the ground. It came close to us and stopped. Oh, how my heart beat! I thought whoever it was would hear my heart beat."
"Oh, what a pretty dress my sister got! I did not get anything, because I hid all the time. I was hiding under some robes. No one knew where I was."

"Late in that fall, there came news that my grandfather was on his way home. Then my father took a great many of his men and went to meet his father, and there came back a runner, saying, that all our people must come together."

subject: reading
prompt:
Which choice most effectively uses data from the graph to complete the example?
A report from an international organization that monitors the numbers of women serving as judges or magistrates on various nations' highest courts, such as the Supreme Court of Justice and the Constitutional Court in Belgium and the Supreme Court in the Philippines, indicates that, other than the countries that had more women on these courts in 2013 than in 2009, for some the number in 2013 was less than that in 2009, and for others it was the same in both years. For instance, the number of women judges and magistrates on high courts in _____
passage: A report from an international organization that monitors the numbers of women serving as judges or magistrates on various nations' highest courts, such as the Supreme Court of Justice and the Constitutional Court in Belgium and the Supreme Court in the Philippines, indicates that, other than the countries that had more women on these courts in 2013 than in 2009, for some the number in 2013 was less than that in 2009, and for others it was the same in both years. For instance, the number of women judges and magistrates on high courts in _____
questionText: Which choice most effectively uses data from the graph to complete the example?

choices.text: the Philippines was lower in 2013 than in 2009, whereas the number in Belgium was the same in 2013 as in 2009.
Belgium was the same in 2013 as in 2009, but it had more women on its high courts than either Guatemala or the Philippines did in 2013.
Guatemala was greater in 2013 than in 2009, whereas the number in the Philippines was lower in 2013 than in 2009.
Guatemala was greater in 2013 than in 2009, whereas the number in Belgium was the same in 2013 as in 2009.

subject: reading
prompt:
Which quotation from a publication by a researcher would most effectively support the student's claim?
A student is writing an essay on the subject of cultured meat, which is grown in a laboratory and is intended to help reduce the number of livestock harvested for food. The student wishes to make the case that people have mixed feelings about cultured meat that may be a barrier to its worldwide adoption.
passage: A student is writing an essay on the subject of cultured meat, which is grown in a laboratory and is intended to help reduce the number of livestock harvested for food. The student wishes to make the case that people have mixed feelings about cultured meat that may be a barrier to its worldwide adoption.
questionText: Which quotation from a publication by a researcher would most effectively support the student's claim?

choices.text: "Consumers tend to believe that using less packaging when selling meat products in stores would have a significant effect on the environment."

"Many consumers who were surveyed believed that cultured meat would be good for the environment if widely available, but few of those same consumers were willing to try eating cultured meat themselves."
"During the process of creating lab-grown meat, cells are kept at the typical body temperatures of cows and other animals in order to speed up growth."
"Advocates of lab-grown meat claim that it's better for the environment than conventional meat because it requires less land."

subject: reading
prompt:
Which choice most logically completes the text?
Evan MacLean and colleagues evaluated behavioral and genetic data from over 14,000 dogs, representing more than 100 breeds, and found that certain similarities in behavior between breeds correspond to genetic similarities between those breeds, suggesting a genetic basis for breed differences in behavior. This was the case for both energy and excitability but was especially pronounced for trainability, which can be seen when a dog demonstrates willingness to fetch objects. A different study found that the bulldog and the French bulldog breeds exhibit similar trainability behavior, suggesting that _____
passage: Evan MacLean and colleagues evaluated behavioral and genetic data from over 14,000 dogs, representing more than 100 breeds, and found that certain similarities in behavior between breeds correspond to genetic similarities between those breeds, suggesting a genetic basis for breed differences in behavior. This was the case for both energy and excitability but was especially pronounced for trainability, which can be seen when a dog demonstrates willingness to fetch objects. A different study found that the bulldog and the French bulldog breeds exhibit similar trainability behavior, suggesting that _____
questionText: Which choice most logically completes the text?

choices.text: bulldogs and French bulldogs show a greater tendency toward trainability than most other dog breeds do.
individual bulldogs likely display higher levels of trainability than individual French bulldogs.
their similarities with respect to that behavior could result from a shared aspect of their genetics.
the two breeds will likely become less genetically similar over time.

subject: reading
prompt:
Which choice most logically completes the text?
Canada has classified the round goby as an invasive species that could harm some of the country's native species. But researchers Alejandro Camacho and Jason McLachlan have pointed out that "invasive" and "native" are labels that describe temporary circumstances. Changes in Earth's climate may force animals from their current ranges. Climate changes may also create good habitats in areas where a species couldn't live previously. In the case of Canada, these observations suggest that _____
passage: Canada has classified the round goby as an invasive species that could harm some of the country's native species. But researchers Alejandro Camacho and Jason McLachlan have pointed out that "invasive" and "native" are labels that describe temporary circumstances. Changes in Earth's climate may force animals from their current ranges. Climate changes may also create good habitats in areas where a species couldn't live previously. In the case of Canada, these observations suggest that _____
questionText: Which choice most logically completes the text?

choices.text: the country was previously home to some round gobies but they were outcompeted by invading species.
the country's designation of the round goby as invasive may be appropriate now but not in the future.
it's useful at present for the country to distinguish between invasive and native species in some instances but not in the case of the round goby.
even if Earth's climate doesn't change in the way scientists predict, the round goby will likely establish itself in the country.

subject: reading
prompt:
Which choice most logically completes the text?
In May of 1987, Malaysia liberalized its stock market, meaning that it began allowing foreign individuals and businesses to invest money in Malaysian companies. This was part of a wave of stock market liberalizations around the world—Bangladesh in 1991, Kenya in 1995, and so on. The standard view among economists at the time was that liberalization would make it easier for companies to raise money from investors. Economist Peter Blair Henry examined the economies of 11 countries that were part of the liberalization wave and found that, on average, companies based in those countries received significant increases in investment in the three years following liberalization, suggesting that ______
passage: In May of 1987, Malaysia liberalized its stock market, meaning that it began allowing foreign individuals and businesses to invest money in Malaysian companies. This was part of a wave of stock market liberalizations around the world—Bangladesh in 1991, Kenya in 1995, and so on. The standard view among economists at the time was that liberalization would make it easier for companies to raise money from investors. Economist Peter Blair Henry examined the economies of 11 countries that were part of the liberalization wave and found that, on average, companies based in those countries received significant increases in investment in the three years following liberalization, suggesting that ______
questionText: Which choice most logically completes the text?

choices.text: companies in Bangladesh experienced a greater increase in investment following liberalization than did companies in Malaysia.
economists who held the standard view of liberalization failed to anticipate some serious negative effects of liberalization.
companies did not benefit from liberalization until at least three years after liberalization occurred.
empirical evidence was consistent with the scholarly consensus about the consequences of liberalization.
